one_line_takeaway: AgenTRIM reframes LLM-agent tool security as balancing 'excessive vs insufficient agency' and enforces per-step least-privilege tool access via an offline execution-verified tool inventory plus a runtime orchestrator, cutting AgentDojo IPI attack success to ~2% while slightly improving utility at only ~1.85x latency.
key_finding: On AgentDojo IPI (important-instructions attack, GPT-4o), AgenTRIM cuts overall attack success rate to 2.3% from the baseline agent's 24.3% while RAISING utility: 77.1% benign utility (vs 71.1% baseline) and 69.2% utility-under-attack (vs 60.4%), reaching near-zero ASR on Workspace/Travel/Banking suites (0/0/0.9%) with 12.7% remaining on Slack. Averaged over five attack styles (Table 8) its ASR is 1.1% vs the baseline's 10.8%. It is closest to the ideal (high utility, low ASR) among all four AgentDojo benchmark defenses and, versus SOTA IPI defenses (CaMeL, MELON, Progent, AgentArmor), gives the best security-utility trade-off with the smallest relative utility drop under attack and only ~1.85x latency / ~2x cost, vs ~3-9x for CaMeL and AgentArmor (this head-to-head is qualitative, per Fig. 6). The offline extractor is highly reliable as an inventory-classification task: on 500 ReAct configs it hits precision 1.0, recall 0.997, and ZERO fabrication rate (perfect on the AgentDojo/EHRAgent/Travel external agents). For tool-description attacks, sanitizing descriptions drives Shadow-attack ASR to 0% and MPMA ASR down toward random selection. For policy enforcement (1k queries, 3 safety policies), AgenTRIM-with-safety-tools reaches safety-tool F1 0.995, functional F1 0.981, and 0.0 policy-breach rate, whereas the baseline breaches policy in all cases (PBR 1.0).
core_contribution: AgenTRIM (from Fujitsu Research) is a framework for mitigating tool-related security risks in LLM-based agents by 'balancing tool-driven agency' without modifying the agent's internal reasoning. The paper frames tool-related failures as two-sided: 'excessive agency' (agent retains unnecessary/over-permissive tools, enlarging attack surface for indirect prompt injection and tool misuse) and 'insufficient agency' (agent lacks or misidentifies a required tool, causing task failure or missed safety tools). Its own claimed novelty is (1) the first principled framework targeting BOTH excessive and insufficient agency rather than just symptom-level attack-success suppression; (2) an OFFLINE hybrid 'tool extractor' that combines deterministic static code/AST analysis with execution-trace validation and LLM-assisted description generation to produce a verified, risk-labeled tool inventory; and (3) an ONLINE lightweight 'tool orchestrator' that enforces per-step least-privilege tool exposure via deterministic adaptive filtering plus a status-aware 'high-risk judge' that validates high-risk tool calls using only a running task-status summary (isolated from the agent's internal reasoning). Note: commercial static analyzers Agentic Radar (SplxAI) and AgentWiz (RepelloAI) are cited as CONTRASTS (static, code-only, no runtime mediation), not as part of AgenTRIM; and the corrupted input abstract describing a 'dependency graph-based defense' actually belongs to a cited work (An et al., IPIGuard, EMNLP 2025), not this paper.
threat_model: Adversary controls untrusted content that an agent's tools ingest or the tool metadata itself. Three concrete threat classes are evaluated: (1) Indirect prompt injection (IPI) - hidden instructions embedded in tool outputs / external content (web pages, files, emails) that hijack the agent into invoking tools maliciously (e.g., an injected file causing a send_money transfer to an attacker, the paper's Fig. 2 qualitative example). (2) Tool-description attacks that weaponize the tool metadata rather than outputs: MPMA (preference-manipulation of tool names/descriptions to bias tool selection) and Shadow/tool-poisoning attacks (covert instructions in descriptions inducing unintended tool chaining), plus non-malicious erroneous/misleading descriptions. (3) Policy violations from insufficient agency - functional tools executed without a required safety tool. AgenTRIM assumes it can run the agent in a controlled/isolated environment offline for trace-based validation and can intercept the LLM-tool loop at runtime; it does not alter the agent's reasoning and treats the tool layer (permissions + descriptions) as the locus of failure.
limitations: Author-stated: (1) Offline validation requires executing the agent in a controlled/isolated environment; porting to a new agentic framework needs lightweight framework-specific tracing/logging integration (a one-time setup cost). (2) Effectiveness of the runtime orchestrator depends on an appropriate low/high-risk tool partition; the default read-only-vs-environment-modifying split works in their evaluations but some domains need custom risk definitions, and misclassification affects the utility-safety trade-off (adjustable by re-labeling). (3) Runtime mediation adds overhead: ~1.85x latency and ~2x cost vs baseline. Reviewer-noted gaps: cross-method comparisons against CaMeL/MELON/Progent/AgentArmor are largely qualitative with competitor figures taken from their own papers under differing models/baselines (the paper itself flags heterogeneity - e.g., CaMeL reports no GPT-4o results, AgentArmor code was unavailable), so absolute placements are approximate; the high-risk judge and status manager themselves rely on LLM calls whose own robustness under adversarial status text is not stressed; Slack-suite residual ASR (12.7%) shows the approach is not uniformly airtight.

datasets_used: AgentDojo benchmark (4 suites: Workspace, Travel, Banking, Slack; 97 benign tasks and 629 injected variants; attack styles include direct, ignore_previous, system-message, tool-knowledge, important-instructions)

Custom 20-tool pool (LangChain web-search/SQL read + read-write, custom tools, Gmail list/read/send/delete, two MCP servers) used to instantiate 500 ReAct agent configurations across LangGraph, AutoGen, CrewAI
EHR-agent (Shi et al. 2024) and a CrewAI travel/trip-planner agent as external-agent extractor tests
MCP-based tool-description attack sets: MPMA and Shadow attacks, 50 realistic queries per functionality on LangGraph/AutoGen ReAct agents
Policy-integration synthetic suite: 10 functional tools, 3 safety policies, 1,000 generated queries
quantitative_results: AgentDojo important-instructions attack (overall, mean of 5 runs): AgenTRIM ASR 2.3% vs baseline 24.3%; benign utility 77.1% vs 71.1%; utility-under-attack 69.2% vs 60.4%
Per-suite ASR for AgenTRIM: Workspace 0%, Travel 0%, Banking 0.9%, Slack 12.7%
Averaged over 5 attack styles (Table 8): AgenTRIM ASR 1.1% and UUA 69.8% vs baseline ASR 10.8%

Offline tool extractor on 500 ReAct configs: precision 1.0, recall 0.997, miss rate 0.003, F1 0.999, accuracy 0.998, fabrication rate 0.0 (perfect 1.0 across AgentDojo/EHRAgent/Travel external agents)
Extractor ablation: code+trace = precision 1.0 / recall 0.997 / fabrication 0.0, whereas code+no-validation inflates fabrication to 3.462 and no-initial-list+trace collapses recall to 0.152
LLM ablation (30 ReAct instances): recall GPT-4o 0.99, GPT-4o-mini 0.96, Mistral Small 3.1 0.96, Command A 0.93; all with precision 1.00 and fabrication 0.0

Policy integration (1k queries): AgenTRIM w/ safety tools -> functional F1 0.981, safety precision 0.989, safety recall 1.000, safety F1 0.995, policy-breach rate 0.0; baseline functional F1 0.318, PBR 1.0; baseline w/ safety tools safety F1 only 0.265
Tool-description attacks: Shadow-attack ASR driven to 0% after description sanitization; MPMA ASR reduced toward random selection
Overhead: ~1.85x latency (baseline 4.92 s/task vs AgenTRIM 8.84 s/task, L~=1.8x) and ~2x cost; tool-usage rate ~25% overall, high-risk tools used at 100% when exposed
Competitor calibration BASELINES (each from its own paper, NOT defended results): AgentArmor baseline util 73%/ASR 17%, Progent baseline 79%/39.9%, MELON GPT-4o baseline 80%/51%
baselines_compared: Four AgentDojo benchmark defenses: PI Detector, tool-output formatter (Delimiter), Tool Filter, Repeat Prompt
SOTA IPI defenses (qualitative comparison, numbers from their papers): CaMeL, MELON, Progent, AgentArmor
